---
title: "Output Formats"
subtitle: "HTML, Word, PDF, and LaTeX"
---

## Purpose

This chapter covers rendering a Quarto document to different output formats. The same `.qmd` source file can produce HTML, Word, and PDF output — but each format has specific YAML options and some practical differences to be aware of.

## Rendering formats

Specify output formats in the YAML `format:` block:
````yaml
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    keep-tex: true
  docx:
    reference-doc: custom-reference.docx
````

Render all formats at once:
````bash
quarto render manuscript.qmd
````

Or a single format:
````bash
quarto render manuscript.qmd --to docx
````

## HTML

HTML is the most flexible output format and the easiest to share.
````yaml
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
    theme: flatly
````

::: callout-note
## Self-contained HTML with `embed-resources`
By default, Quarto renders HTML with external dependencies (CSS, JavaScript, figures) stored in a separate folder. Setting `embed-resources: true` bundles everything into a single `.html` file, making it easy to share without worrying about missing assets.

`embed-resources: true` increases file size and slows rendering slightly. For a book or website hosted online, leave it as `false`.
:::

## Word

Word output is useful for sharing with co-authors or for journal submission.
````yaml
format:
  docx:
    reference-doc: custom-reference.docx
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
````

A reference `.docx` sets the styles (fonts, margins, heading levels) for the output. Generate a default one with:
````bash
quarto pandoc -o custom-reference.docx --print-default-data-file reference.docx
````

Then open it in Word and modify the styles to match your journal's requirements.

::: callout-warning
## Citations in Word
Citations are rendered as plain formatted text in `.docx` output — they are not live fields linked to your `.bib` file. Any edits made directly to citations in Word will be overwritten on re-render. Always treat the `.qmd` as the source of truth.

If co-authors need to edit citations in Word, consider using Zotero's Word plugin for the final submission copy.
:::

## PDF

PDF output is produced via LaTeX. The most important options:
````yaml
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article   # or scrartcl, apa7
    papersize: a4
    fontsize: 12pt
    geometry: margin=2.5cm
    linestretch: 1.5
    number-sections: true
    keep-tex: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{longtable}
        \usepackage{microtype}
````

::: callout-tip
## keep-tex
Setting `keep-tex: true` saves the intermediate `.tex` file alongside the PDF. This is useful if you need to make final tweaks in Overleaf before submission, or if the journal requires a raw `.tex` file.
:::

## Format comparison

| Feature | HTML | PDF | Word |
|---|---|---|---|
| Citations | ✅ from `.bib` | ✅ from `.bib` | ✅ rendered as text |
| Live `.bib` link | ✅ | depends on `cite-method` | ❌ |
| Cross-references | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Equations | ✅ MathJax | ✅ native LaTeX | ⚠️ limited |
| Table formatting | `gt` | `kableExtra` | `flextable` |
| Custom styles | CSS | LaTeX | reference `.docx` |
| Self-contained | `embed-resources: true` | always | always |

## APA 7 and `.tex` output {#sec-apa7}

These issues are only relevant if you want to edit the `.tex` file after rendering (e.g., in Overleaf). If you render directly to PDF, Word, or HTML from Quarto, citations will be formatted correctly regardless of which option you use.

For `.tex` editing workflows, there is no perfect solution:

**citeproc (default)**  
Uses `csl: apa.csl`. Gives the most accurate APA 7 output but resolves citations to plain text in the `.tex` file, so there are no live `\cite{}` commands for Overleaf.

**natbib + apacite**  
Gives live `\cite{}` commands in the `.tex` file. However, `apacite` targets APA 6th edition and has known APA 7 formatting issues (author limits, DOI formatting).

**biblatex + biblatex-apa**  
The most APA 7-compliant option with live citations. Requires `biber` as the backend, which must be set manually in Overleaf and is more complex to set up.

| Option | APA 7 accuracy | Live `.tex` citations | Overleaf |
|---|---|---|---|
| citeproc + `apa.csl` | ✅ best | ❌ | ⚠️ upload `.tex` only |
| natbib + `apacite` | ⚠️ APA 6 | ✅ | ✅ with `.bib` |
| biblatex + `biblatex-apa` | ✅ good | ✅ | ✅ set biber in compiler |

Choose based on your priority: APA 7 accuracy or Overleaf compatibility.

To switch from the default citeproc:
````yaml
# natbib
format:
  pdf:
    keep-tex: true
    cite-method: natbib
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage[natbibapa]{apacite}

# biblatex
format:
  pdf:
    keep-tex: true
    cite-method: biblatex
    biblio-style: apa
````

## Exercises

### Exercise 1 — Render to all formats

Add HTML, PDF, and Word format blocks to your exercise `.qmd` YAML and render all three. Compare the outputs — what looks different?

### Exercise 2 — Self-contained HTML

Add `embed-resources: true` to your HTML format block and re-render. Check the file size difference between the self-contained and non-self-contained versions.

### Exercise 3 — Word with a reference document

1. Generate a default reference `.docx`:
````bash
quarto pandoc -o custom-reference.docx --print-default-data-file reference.docx
````
2. Open it in Word and change the body font to Times New Roman 12pt
3. Point your YAML to it with `reference-doc: custom-reference.docx` and re-render

### Exercise 4 — keep-tex and Overleaf

1. Add `keep-tex: true` to your PDF format block and render
2. Open the `.tex` file and find your citations — are they `\cite{}` commands or plain text?
3. Upload the `.tex` to a new Overleaf project and compile

### Exercise 5 — natbib or biblatex

Switch your citation backend to either `natbib` or `biblatex` and re-render to PDF. Check:

- Do citations appear correctly in the PDF?
- Open the `.tex` file — are citations now live `\cite{}` commands?
- Upload to Overleaf — do citations resolve?

## Chapter slides
````{=html}
<a href="../slides/08_formats.html" class="btn btn-outline-primary" target="_blank">
  Open slides ↗
</a>
````
